title: KNN Diabetes Prediction Capstone
text:
Diabetes is a chronic health condition that can lead to serious complications when it is not identified early. Because early detection improves long‑term outcomes, machine learning methods have become increasingly useful for supporting clinical decision‑making. These models can identify patterns in health data that may not be immediately visible through traditional analysis, making them valuable tools for screening and risk assessment (Xing & Bei, 2020; Nain & Tomar, 2023).
In this project, I applied the K‑Nearest Neighbors (KNN) algorithm to the Pima Indians Diabetes dataset to predict whether an individual is likely to have diabetes. KNN is a distance‑based classification method that assigns a label to a new observation by comparing it to the most similar cases in the training data (Zhang et al., 2018). Because the algorithm relies directly on the training dataset when making predictions, its performance depends heavily on data quality, preprocessing, and the selection of the tuning parameter k (Zhang, 2022).
KNN uses distance calculations—typically Euclidean distance—to determine similarity between observations. This makes preprocessing essential, since variables with larger numeric ranges can disproportionately influence the distance metric if the data is not standardized. The choice of k also plays a major role in model performance. Smaller values may lead to overfitting, while larger values may smooth out meaningful distinctions. KNN has been applied in areas such as finance, forecasting, and medical diagnosis due to its simplicity and its ability to perform well when properly tuned (Alkhatib et al., 2013).
The purpose of this project was to evaluate the predictive performance of KNN on the Pima Indians Diabetes dataset, compare it to baseline models, and examine how preprocessing and tuning influence classification accuracy. The broader goal was to determine whether KNN can serve as an effective and interpretable tool for diabetes risk prediction.

title: KNN Diabetes Prediction Capstone
text:
Click here to view the full presentation
Using the K-Nearest Neighbors Algorithm to Predict Diabetes Outcomes
Jennifer Becerra
IDC 6940 – Capstone Project
University of West Florida

title: KNN Diabetes Prediction Capstone
text:
Abstract
This project applies the K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) machine learning algorithm to the Pima Indians Diabetes dataset to predict whether a patient is likely to have diabetes. After preprocessing, scaling, and tuning the hyperparameter k, the final KNN model achieved a test AUC of 0.8601 and an accuracy of 0.7843, outperforming Logistic Regression and Decision Tree models and performing competitively with Random Forest. These results demonstrate that KNN is a strong and interpretable model for medical classification tasks when data is properly prepared.

title: KNN Diabetes Prediction Capstone
text:
Diabetes is a chronic health condition that can lead to serious complications when it is not identified early. Because early detection improves long‑term outcomes, machine learning methods have become increasingly useful for supporting clinical decision‑making. These models can identify patterns in health data that may not be immediately visible through traditional analysis, making them valuable tools for screening and risk assessment (Xing & Bei, 2020; Nain & Tomar, 2023).
In this project, I applied the K‑Nearest Neighbors (KNN) algorithm to the Pima Indians Diabetes dataset to predict whether an individual is likely to have diabetes. KNN is a distance‑based classification method that assigns a label to a new observation by comparing it to the most similar cases in the training data (Zhang et al., 2018). Because the algorithm relies directly on the training dataset when making predictions, its performance depends heavily on data quality, preprocessing, and the selection of the tuning parameter k (Zhang, 2022).
KNN uses distance calculations—typically Euclidean distance—to determine similarity between observations. This makes preprocessing essential, since variables with larger numeric ranges can disproportionately influence the distance metric if the data is not standardized. The choice of k also plays a major role in model performance. Smaller values may lead to overfitting, while larger values may smooth out meaningful distinctions. KNN has been applied in areas such as finance, forecasting, and medical diagnosis due to its simplicity and its ability to perform well when properly tuned (Alkhatib et al., 2013).
The purpose of this project was to evaluate the predictive performance of KNN on the Pima Indians Diabetes dataset, compare it to baseline models, and examine how preprocessing and tuning influence classification accuracy. The broader goal was to determine whether KNN can serve as an effective and interpretable tool for diabetes risk prediction.

title: KNN Diabetes Prediction Capstone
text:
0.3 Methods

0.3.1 Dataset
The dataset contains 768 observations of female patients of Pima Indian heritage. Each record includes eight predictors—such as glucose level, BMI, blood pressure, and age—along with a binary outcome variable indicating diabetes status (0 = no diabetes, 1 = diabetes). The dataset is commonly used as a benchmark for evaluating classification models in medical prediction tasks (Xing & Bei, 2020).


0.3.2 Preprocessing
Several predictors contained zero values that are not physiologically meaningful, such as zero insulin or zero blood pressure. These values were treated as missing and replaced using median imputation. All predictors were then standardized through centering and scaling to ensure that each variable contributed equally to the distance calculations used by KNN. Without standardization, variables with larger numeric ranges would dominate the similarity measure and reduce model performance (Zhang, 2022).


0.3.3 Train–Test Split
The dataset was divided into an 80% training set and a 20% testing set. The training set was used for model fitting and hyperparameter tuning, while the test set was reserved for final evaluation to provide an unbiased estimate of model performance.


0.3.4 Models and Tuning
Four models were trained for comparison: KNN, logistic regression, decision tree, and random forest. The KNN model was tuned across values of k ranging from 1 to 25 using 10‑fold cross‑validation. The primary tuning metric was the Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC), with accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity also examined to provide a more complete assessment of performance.


0.3.5 Evaluation Metrics
Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, AUC, the confusion matrix, sensitivity, and specificity. Sensitivity is particularly important in clinical screening because failing to identify true positive cases can delay diagnosis and treatment (Nain & Tomar, 2023). AUC was used as the primary metric because it reflects the model’s ability to distinguish between diabetic and non‑diabetic individuals across all classification thresholds.

title: KNN Diabetes Prediction Capstone
text:
3. Analysis and Results

Model Performance



Model
Accuracy
AUC




KNN (k = 21)
0.7843
0.8601


Logistic Regression
0.804
0.757


Decision Tree
0.758
0.709


Random Forest
0.810
0.775



KNN achieved the highest AUC and performed competitively with Random Forest in accuracy. Logistic Regression performed moderately well, while the Decision Tree model showed signs of overfitting.


Confusion Matrix Interpretation
The KNN confusion matrix highlights clinically meaningful trade-offs:

True positives: enable early intervention

False negatives: risk delayed diagnosis

False positives: may lead to unnecessary follow-up testing

True negatives: avoid unnecessary clinical workup

Given the goal of screening, sensitivity is particularly important, and KNN provided a favorable balance between sensitivity and specificity.

title: KNN Diabetes Prediction Capstone
text:
4. Discussion
The results demonstrate that KNN is a strong and interpretable model for predicting diabetes onset when predictors are properly cleaned and standardized. Its performance aligns with findings in prior medical machine-learning research, which emphasize the importance of preprocessing for distance-based algorithms.
Random Forest performed similarly in accuracy but with lower AUC, suggesting that KNN captured clinically relevant structure in the data more effectively. Logistic Regression remained competitive, indicating that linear relationships contribute predictive value. The Decision Tree model underperformed, likely due to its tendency to overfit small datasets.
The tuning process showed that larger values of k (around 20–25) produced more stable and generalizable predictions, reducing noise from outliers.

title: KNN Diabetes Prediction Capstone
text:
7. Conclusion
This project successfully applied the K-Nearest Neighbors algorithm to predict diabetes outcomes using the Pima Indians Diabetes dataset. After preprocessing and tuning, KNN achieved strong predictive performance, with an AUC of 0.8601 and accuracy of 0.7843, outperforming or matching more complex models.
These findings support the use of KNN as an effective and interpretable tool for clinical screening tasks. With further refinement and validation, KNN-based models could help identify high-risk individuals earlier and support preventive healthcare decisions.

title: KNN Diabetes Prediction Capstone
text:
8. References
Xing, Y., & Bei, Y. (2020). Medical Health Big Data Classification Based on KNN Classification Algorithm. IEEE Access.
Zhang, X., Li, M., Zong, X., Zhu, X., & Wang, R. (2018). Efficient kNN Classification With Different Numbers of Nearest Neighbors. IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems.
Alkhatib, K., Najadat, H., Hmeidi, I., & Shatnawi, M. K. A. (2013). Stock price prediction using k-nearest neighbor (kNN) algorithm. International Journal of Business, Humanities and Technology, 3(3), 32–44
Nain, S., & Tomar, S. (2023). Medical image prediction for diagnosis of breast cancer using machine learning. AIP Conference Proceedings, 2853(1), 020140.
Zhang, S. (2022). Challenges in KNN Classification. IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering, 34(10), 4663–4675.

Jennifer Becerra
IDC 6940 – Capstone Project
University of West Florida

title: Using the K-Nearest Neighbors Algorithm to Predict Diabetes Outcomes
text:
Objective
Primary objective
- Build and evaluate a K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) classifier to predict diabetes onset using the Pima Indians dataset and compare KNN to baseline models (Xing 2020).
Why this matters
- Early identification of individuals at high risk for diabetes enables targeted screening, lifestyle interventions, and timely clinical follow-up, reducing complications and healthcare costs (Zhang 2022).
Specific aims
- Preprocess clinical predictors (impute, standardize) while preserving the outcome variable.
- Tune K (1–25) with 10‑fold cross‑validation and report AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity.
- Produce reproducible visuals and interpret model trade-offs for clinical screening.

title: Using the K-Nearest Neighbors Algorithm to Predict Diabetes Outcomes
text:
Diabetes Background and Outcomes
Clinical context

Type 2 diabetes is a progressive metabolic disorder characterized by insulin resistance and impaired insulin secretion.

It increases risk for cardiovascular disease, kidney failure, neuropathy, and vision loss.

Predictive models help identify high‑risk patients earlier than traditional screening alone (Nain 2023).

Outcomes of interest

Primary outcome: diabetes onset (No vs Yes).

Clinical implications:

True positive: early intervention → reduced long‑term complications.

False negative: missed high‑risk patient → delayed care.

False positive: unnecessary follow‑up testing → resource use + anxiety.


Goal: maximize sensitivity for screening while maintaining reasonable specificity.

title: Using the K-Nearest Neighbors Algorithm to Predict Diabetes Outcomes
text:
Dataset Summary

Source: Pima Indians Diabetes dataset (768 records).

Predictors: Pregnancies, Glucose, BloodPressure, SkinThickness, Insulin, BMI, DiabetesPedigreeFunction, Age.

Outcome: Diabetes onset (0 = No, 1 = Yes).

Preprocessing:

Replaced impossible zeros with NA

Median imputation

Standardized predictors

Outcome preserved unscaled

KNN tuned across k = 1–25 using ROC (Zhang 2018).

Baselines: logistic regression, decision tree, random forest

Metrics: AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, confusion matrix

title: Using the K-Nearest Neighbors Algorithm to Predict Diabetes Outcomes
text:
Feature Influence


Standardized variability used as a proxy for influence

Glucose, BMI, and Age show strong clinical relevance (Xing 2020)

title: Using the K-Nearest Neighbors Algorithm to Predict Diabetes Outcomes
text:
Conclusion

The KNN model demonstrated strong predictive ability for diabetes onset when predictors were properly cleaned and standardized, consistent with findings in prior medical ML research (Xing 2020).

Glucose, BMI, and Age emerged as clinically meaningful contributors, aligning with established diabetes risk factors reported in the literature (Nain 2023).

With an AUC of 0.852 and accuracy of 0.810, KNN performed competitively with more complex models, supporting evidence that simple algorithms can be effective for clinical screening tasks (Zhang 2022).

These results highlight the potential of machine‑learning–based screening tools to identify high‑risk individuals earlier and support preventive healthcare decisions.

Future work should explore threshold tuning for screening sensitivity, distance‑weighted KNN, and validation on broader patient populations.

How I Selected k = 21
Tuning Process

Tuned k = 1 to 25
Used 10‑fold cross‑validation
Evaluated each k using AUC (ROC)
k = 21 produced the highest cross‑validated AUC
Balanced bias (small k) and variance (large k)

Visual — Tuning Curve
plot(knn_model, main = “KNN Tuning Curve (k = 1 to 25)”)

title: KNN Diabetes Prediction Capstone
text:
5. Limitations
Although the results are promising, several limitations should be acknowledged:

The dataset is relatively small (768 records), which may limit generalizability.

All patients are from a single population group (Pima Indian women), which restricts demographic diversity.

KNN can be computationally expensive on larger datasets, though this was not an issue here.

The model relies heavily on preprocessing; small changes in scaling or imputation can shift performance.

Only a limited set of models was compared; more advanced methods (e.g., gradient boosting) were not explored.

title: KNN Diabetes Prediction Capstone
text:
6. Future Work
Future work could expand on this project in several ways:

Explore additional distance metrics (Manhattan, Minkowski).

Implement distance-weighted KNN to give closer neighbors more influence.

Perform threshold tuning to maximize sensitivity for clinical screening.

Validate the model on external datasets with more diverse populations.

Compare KNN to more advanced models such as XGBoost or LightGBM.

Incorporate feature selection or dimensionality reduction to improve efficiency.

Jennifer Becerra

title: KNN Diabetes Prediction Capstone
text:
Abstract
This project applies the K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) machine learning algorithm to the Pima Indians Diabetes dataset to predict whether a patient is likely to have diabetes. After preprocessing, scaling, and tuning the hyperparameter k, the final KNN model achieved a test AUC of 0.8601 and an accuracy of 0.7843, outperforming Logistic Regression and Decision Tree models and performing competitively with Random Forest. These results demonstrate that KNN is a strong and interpretable model for medical classification tasks when data is properly prepared.

title: KNN Diabetes Prediction Capstone
text:
1. Introduction
The K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) algorithm is one of the simplest and most intuitive supervised machine learning methods used for both classification and regression. It requires two key components: a distance metric and a defined value of K.
The distance metric measures how far a query point is from other data points. Common distance metrics include Euclidean, Manhattan, and Minkowski distance. These distances determine which points are considered “neighbors” of the query point.
The value of K determines how many neighbors are used to classify a new observation. Smaller values of K can lead to overfitting, while larger values may oversmooth the decision boundary. Choosing an appropriate K is therefore essential for model performance.

Strengths of KNN

Easy to understand and implement

Minimal hyperparameters

Adaptable — stores all training data and updates predictions dynamically



Weaknesses of KNN

Lazy learner — no model is built ahead of time

Computationally expensive for large datasets

Sensitive to feature scaling

Susceptible to the peaking phenomenon



Applications of KNN
KNN is used in: - Recommendation systems
- Missing data imputation
- Finance (forecasting, anomaly detection)
- Healthcare (risk prediction, diagnosis support)
In this paper, I apply the KNN algorithm to a dataset of potential diabetic patients to evaluate its effectiveness in medical prediction.

title: KNN Diabetes Prediction Capstone
text:
2. Methods

Dataset
The Pima Indians Diabetes dataset contains 768 patient records with the following variables:
Pregnancies, Glucose, Blood Pressure, Skin Thickness, Insulin, BMI, Diabetes Pedigree Function, Age, Outcome (0 = no diabetes, 1 = diabetes)


Preprocessing
Several features contained zero values that were not physiologically possible (e.g., zero insulin). These were replaced with missing values and imputed using median imputation. All predictor variables were standardized using centering and scaling to ensure KNN performed correctly, since distance-based models are highly sensitive to variable magnitude.


Model Training and Evaluation
The dataset was split into: - 80% training
- 20% testing
Models evaluated: - K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)
- Logistic Regression
- Decision Tree
- Random Forest
KNN was tuned across k = 1 to k = 25 using 10-fold cross-validation.
Performance metrics: - Accuracy
- AUC
- Confusion Matrix
- Sensitivity and Specificity


Model Development Process and Tuning Trials
Before arriving at the final KNN model, I went through several rounds of experimentation to understand how the algorithm behaved with this dataset. My first step was to build a simple baseline KNN model using the default settings in R. This gave me a starting point, but the accuracy and AUC were lower than expected, which confirmed that tuning the hyperparameter k would be necessary.
I initially tested values of k from 1 to 25, which is a common range for datasets of this size. In my early trials, the tuning process suggested that k = 23 produced the best accuracy. At that point, however, my preprocessing pipeline was not fully finalized. I had replaced zero values with missing values, but I later realized that I needed to re-check the imputation and re-standardize the predictors to ensure that all variables contributed equally to the distance calculations.
After improving the preprocessing steps — specifically re-running median imputation and re-scaling the predictors — I repeated the entire tuning process. With the updated pipeline, the performance curve shifted slightly, and k = 21 consistently produced the highest cross-validated AUC. This change made sense because KNN is extremely sensitive to scaling and distance calculations, so even small adjustments in preprocessing can influence which value of k performs best.
To make sure the tuning results were stable, I re-ran the cross-validation multiple times and checked whether the best k changed across runs. The performance plateaued between k = 19 and k = 23, which is typical when the model reaches a stable region. I ultimately selected k = 21 because it offered the best balance between sensitivity, specificity, and AUC, which is important for a clinical screening context.
Once the final KNN model was selected, I compared it to three baseline models — Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, and Random Forest — using the same 80/20 train/test split. This comparison helped confirm that KNN was not only performing well on its own but was also competitive with more complex models.

Confusion Matrix Interpretation

True positives: enable early intervention

False negatives: risk delayed diagnosis

False positives: may lead to unnecessary follow-up testing

True negatives: avoid unnecessary clinical workup

Given the goal of screening, sensitivity is particularly important, and KNN provided a favorable balance between sensitivity and specificity.

4. Discussion
The results demonstrate that KNN is a strong and interpretable model for predicting diabetes onset when predictors are properly cleaned and standardized. Its performance aligns with findings in prior medical machine-learning research, which emphasize the importance of preprocessing for distance-based algorithms.
Random Forest performed similarly in accuracy but with lower AUC, suggesting that KNN captured clinically relevant structure in the data more effectively. Logistic Regression remained competitive, indicating that linear relationships contribute predictive value. The Decision Tree model underperformed, likely due to its tendency to overfit small datasets.
The tuning process showed that larger values of k (around 20–25) produced more stable and generalizable predictions, reducing noise from outliers.

title: KNN Diabetes Prediction Capstone
text:
5. Limitations
Although the results are promising, several limitations should be acknowledged:

The dataset is relatively small (768 records), which may limit generalizability.

All patients are from a single population group (Pima Indian women), which restricts demographic diversity.

KNN can be computationally expensive on larger datasets, though this was not an issue here.

The model relies heavily on preprocessing; small changes in scaling or imputation can shift performance.

Only a limited set of models was compared; more advanced methods (e.g., gradient boosting) were not explored.

title: KNN Diabetes Prediction Capstone
text:
6. Future Work
Future work could expand on this project in several ways:

Explore additional distance metrics (Manhattan, Minkowski).

Implement distance-weighted KNN to give closer neighbors more influence.

Perform threshold tuning to maximize sensitivity for clinical screening.

Validate the model on external datasets with more diverse populations.

Compare KNN to more advanced models such as XGBoost or LightGBM.

Incorporate feature selection or dimensionality reduction to improve efficiency.

title: KNN Diabetes Prediction Capstone
text:
7. Conclusion
This project successfully applied the K-Nearest Neighbors algorithm to predict diabetes outcomes using the Pima Indians Diabetes dataset. After preprocessing and tuning, KNN achieved strong predictive performance, with an AUC of 0.8601 and accuracy of 0.7843, outperforming or matching more complex models.
These findings support the use of KNN as an effective and interpretable tool for clinical screening tasks. With further refinement and validation, KNN-based models could help identify high-risk individuals earlier and support preventive healthcare decisions.

Before modeling, I reviewed the cleaned dataset to understand the structure and identify patterns that might influence prediction. After preprocessing, the dataset contained 768 observations with no missing values. Prior research has shown that glucose and BMI are strong predictors of diabetes risk, which aligns with the patterns observed in this dataset (Xing & Bei, 2020).
Key patterns observed:

Diabetic patients tend to have higher glucose and BMI

Age is moderately higher in diabetic cases

Insulin and skin thickness show high variability

Histograms of glucose and BMI by diabetes outcome

A correlation heatmap of all predictors

A bar chart showing the distribution of diabetic vs. non‑diabetic cases

10.1 Model Tuning
Cross‑validation showed that k = 21 produced the highest AUC. Performance plateaued between k = 19–23, which is consistent with findings that KNN performance stabilizes once the neighborhood size reaches an optimal range (Zhang et al., 2018).

True positives: early identification

title: KNN Diabetes Prediction Capstone
text:
0.2 Background
The Pima Indians Diabetes dataset is widely used in machine‑learning research because it provides a structured set of clinical and demographic variables relevant to diabetes risk. Prior studies have shown that models trained on this dataset can achieve strong predictive performance when appropriate preprocessing and tuning are applied (Xing & Bei, 2020). KNN is particularly sensitive to these steps because it does not build a parametric model; instead, it relies on the structure of the data itself. This makes the algorithm straightforward to implement but also dependent on careful preparation of the input features.
